title: Reading and Use of English – Part 8
description: You are going to read an article about four people who volunteered at a community repair café. For questions 47–56, choose from the people A–D. The people may be chosen more than once.

texts.A:
### Grace, The Welcome Desk Helper
Grace joined the repair café because she liked the idea of keeping useful objects out of landfill, but she soon discovered that the welcome desk was the most delicate place in the room. People arrived carrying broken lamps, toasters and family clocks, often already frustrated. [47]During the first session, she realised that the informal queue was causing arguments because nobody knew whose item had been accepted next.[/47] She introduced numbered cards and a short description sheet for each object. [48]Her years as a hotel receptionist helped her calm disappointed visitors when repairers could not promise an immediate fix.[/48] Grace learned to say no without making people feel foolish for bringing the wrong kind of appliance for that particular afternoon. She also kept a box of small toys for children waiting with parents. By the end of each afternoon, the desk looked messy, but the room felt more patient than when it opened.
texts.B:
### Amir, The Bicycle Repairer
Amir first volunteered after seeing a poster in the window of the independent bike shop where he bought spare brake cables. [49]The poster’s request for someone who could repair bicycles was what brought him to the café.[/49] He expected punctures, loose chains and muddy wheels, but many visitors needed confidence more than expertise or expensive replacement parts. [50]He was surprised that several adults knew the names of parts they were too nervous to touch.[/50] So he began asking owners to hold tools and make the final adjustment themselves. Amir refused to hurry, even when the queue grew, because a rushed repair taught nothing. He remembers one commuter learning to tighten her own brakes and then proudly cycling around the car park twice, laughing. The work changed his view of repairs: he no longer sees them as a service done for people, but as a conversation that gives practical control back.
texts.C:
### Helen, The Sewing Table Volunteer
Helen ran the busy sewing table with a battered machine she had inherited from her aunt. At first, she wrote instructions using words such as “hem allowance” and “bias binding”, because that was how she had learned. [51]After watching visitors nod politely and then make the same mistake, she rewrote the instruction cards in everyday language with small sketches beside each step and a finished example nearby.[/51] The table immediately became less intimidating for nervous beginners. Helen patiently repaired school trousers, torn curtains and one velvet waistcoat that took nearly the whole afternoon. [52]When she replaced the lining of a coat belonging to a visitor’s late father, the owner held it for several minutes before speaking.[/52] Helen says that clothing repairs often carried stories people had not expected to tell in public. She still enjoys neat stitching, but she now thinks listening carefully is as important as matching the thread.
texts.D:
### Paulo, The Electronics Tester
Paulo came to the repair café after throwing away a radio he later learned could have been fixed with a cheap component and ten minutes of patience. [53]His regret about that radio made him volunteer at the electronics table.[/53] He was good with circuits, but the café’s safety rules mattered more than cleverness. [54]Before he opened any device, he had to check that it was unplugged, dry and listed on the café’s approved appliance sheet.[/54] Some visitors were impatient with those checks, so he explained each one aloud, calmly and clearly. [55]To stop tiny screws disappearing, Paulo brought labelled trays with numbered sections for every part removed during a repair.[/55] The system looked fussy until a child bumped the table and nothing was lost. [56]He is now teaching two newer volunteers how to test appliances safely rather than simply repairing items while they watch.[/56] That makes sessions slower, but safer.

questions.question: noticed that an unclear waiting system was creating disagreements?
questions.explanation: Grace realised the informal queue caused arguments because people did not know whose item was next.

questions.question: used experience from hospitality to manage people’s disappointment politely?
questions.explanation: Grace’s hotel receptionist experience helped her calm disappointed visitors.

questions.question: found the volunteer role through a notice in a place connected with their skill?
questions.explanation: Amir saw a poster in the bike shop asking for someone who could repair bicycles.

questions.question: was surprised that people understood terminology but lacked confidence to act?
questions.explanation: Amir found adults knew bike-part names but were nervous to touch them.

questions.question: changed written guidance after noticing that visitors pretended to understand it?
questions.explanation: Helen rewrote instruction cards after visitors nodded but repeated the same mistake.

questions.question: describes an item associated with a dead relative causing a strong silent reaction?
questions.explanation: A visitor held his late father’s repaired coat for several minutes before speaking.

questions.question: was motivated by regretting that they had thrown something repairable away?
questions.explanation: Paulo volunteered because he regretted throwing away a fixable radio.

questions.question: followed checks before opening items to make sure the work was safe?
questions.explanation: Paulo checked that devices were unplugged, dry and approved before opening them.

questions.question: introduced a system to prevent small components being lost during repairs?
questions.explanation: Paulo used labelled trays with numbered sections for removed parts.

questions.question: is teaching others safe procedures rather than letting them just observe?
questions.explanation: Paulo is training newer volunteers to test appliances safely.
